speaker: interviewer
text: It's April the 15th, 1996. Survivor is Henryka Shaw, at birth Schermant. Interviewer's Diane Armstrong. It is Sydney, Australia, language is English, and this is tape one.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm Diane Armstrong. D-I-A-N-E A-R-M-S-T-R-O-N-G. It's April the 15th, 1996, and I'm interviewing Mrs. Henryka Schermant Shaw in Sydney, Australia. The language will be English. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Could you tell me your name and spell it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Henryka Shaw. H-E-N-R-Y-K-A S-H-A-W.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Schermant. S-C-H-E-R-M-A-N-T.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your date of birth?

speaker: interviewer
text: And your age?

speaker: interviewee
text: About 60-something-- eight, nine. Nine, I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: 69. Is that right?

speaker: interviewee
text: It is.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Cracow, Podgorze.

speaker: interviewer
text: What country?

speaker: interviewee
text: Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: What part of the city was that? Was that a Jewish area?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was not. It was rather very much non-Jewish area. Podgorze was beyond the bridge, and that was not a Jewish area. Jewish area was more in the heart of Cracow.

speaker: interviewer
text: What work did your father do?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was a technical engineer. He had an office in the city. And he used to import parts of machinery from Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any brothers and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I did. I had a sister-- I have a sister-- and a brother I had who perished in Russia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they younger or older than you?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister is older-- six years older than I am. And my brother was four years older. I was the youngest.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your life like in Podgorze, in Cracow, before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very sedate, very much [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] ordinary. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] We had traditional holidays. We have kept traditional Jewish holidays. We all went to school. My mother used to go to the office to help my father. Otherwise she stayed at home-- prepared the dinner. We used to have dinner in the-- 2:00 or so. Everyone went to school only half a day, not like it is in Australia. So she always was home for us when we returned from school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a religious household?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really, no. I would say we were very assimilated. We had very Polish names. And my father was very assimilated Jew. But we did have religious holidays. We observed religious holidays.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any of them?

speaker: interviewee
text: All of them. All of them. In particular, Passover, which we had now-- the whole family would come to us, because my father was the oldest brother. We would open the doors of another room, and there was a table going right through. And everybody-- aunties and uncles and their children-- we were all together.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a big family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, fairly big. My auntie had a son. She was separated from her husband. There was another uncle-- a younger brother with a wife and three children. We were very often together, especially on holidays. My two aunties lived in the same house. It was a family house belonging to us. I even remember my grandmother was living in the same house. And unfortunately, she passed away before the war. But I do have recollection of her stature and the way she spoke. Very much like my father-- tall, very distinguished-looking lady.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your brother have a bar mitzvah that you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, my brother did have bar mitzvah. Very vaguely, I remember it was celebrated in temple. It was very close to Hebrew Gymnasium where he used to attend. And so did my sister, and she completed Hebrew Gymnasium before the war. That's where we celebrated his bar mitzvah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What school did you go to?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to school in Podgorze. First few years I went to a school called Jadwiga Dabrowska-- Dabrowski-- it was called Dabrowski school. And later I went to Henryk Sienkiewicz school, in Podgorze, very close by.

speaker: interviewer
text: What memories do you have of school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the first school was very pleasant, very small, and I got on very well with the teachers. I liked them, and they liked me. And, I would say, because of my height, they very often allowed me to look after the class while they were busy in office or doing something, which sort of pleased me. But later on, when I went to Henryk Sienkiewicz school, I have less pleasant memories.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, one of the school teachers-- it was a very impersonal school-- larger. And the girls were less known to me. The school was [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] close to where we live, and yet perhaps one or two girls I was friendly with, and I did miss the friends from the first school. At one stage, a teacher-- her name was Miss [INAUDIBLE]-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] during the History class, made a statement that Jews are [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] bastards. Her expression was [? dranie, ?] which I feel this is more or less the same. To that I have stood up, and spoke up for the Jews. I was very Jewish. And I made a point, telling her that non-Jews can also be not so very nice. So it had nothing to do with the religion. But, of course, a great discussion evolved in the class. And I was very provoked. All sorts of things were said by the girls and the teacher. And I saw I was losing the--

speaker: interviewer
text: What sort of things were they saying?

speaker: interviewee
text: The usual thing, that the Jews were not very good, and they were exploiting their mothers. Their mothers worked for the Jews. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it was very annoying, because I felt my mother also had help at home, and she has access to anything and everything. She was treated like family. And I felt that everybody else did similarly. And if they didn't, it's because they weren't very nice people, but not that they were Jews. So I said a little ditty, which was not very pleasant and not very nice. But I was totally provoked.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the ditty, do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I remember very well. I'm just embarrassed to tell you. 'Who doesn't care for Jews can kiss their bottom.'

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: But it rhymed very well in Polish. And it wasn't a pleasant expression.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did the teacher react? What was the reaction of the teacher?

speaker: interviewee
text: Absolutely flabbergasted that I dared to say something like that. And so was I. I didn't think I would come out with something like that. But I was totally provoked. And I was told to take my school case and go home and to bring my parents. I came home. I had to tell my parents, because otherwise I wouldn't be able to go back to school. I remember I came with my parents, both of them, to the headmistress's office. And I was sitting at the back. Nobody asked me anything. I wasn't allowed to say a word. I couldn't put my point of view. And my parents were only concerned that I should be allowed to come back to school. I remember I never went back to that school, or any other school, for that matter. That was the end of my formal education.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: The war came, 6-- 6 or 9 of September-- when was it, exactly? In September, '39.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how old were you when you defied the teacher and made your comment?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say about 13.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that common, for schoolgirls to speak up like that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, certainly not the way I have spoken up. One didn't speak like that. Today, you say all those words and lots of other four-letter words. But not in Poland before the war, 50-odd years ago. It was a disaster. I certainly would have been expelled from school and any other school. I wouldn't have access to school-- certainly not in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did your parents react, that you spoke up like that?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a Tisha B'av. Tisha B'av is a holiday, Jewish holiday, that everybody cries because the temple was destroyed.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that's how they reacted.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's how they reacted. Nobody asked me-- nor my mother, nor my father asked me. They probably understood that I was justified, but they were disappointed that I used that language. Because that was the worst I knew then. And they didn't think that their daughter would speak like that, certainly not in the class, to the teacher.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what kind of girl were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, very nice. [LAUGHS] No, I was very timid. Very shy. I have been always very tall, very thin, not particularly good-looking-- at least that's what I thought. Some people agreed with me. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Lank, poorly developed-- no bust, no-- no figure.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was really inhibited. I really was. That was something that-- everybody was surprised that I dared to out up with something like that. So where did that come from?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we live in that area, that the language was used like that. But not in school. Not in front of the teachers. So it was a common word to use. Even my children and grandchildren in Australia know that word. It's a lovely word. You know, like, little 'botty'? It's a nice, sweet word-- not-- not so terrible. But then, it was. And certainly to tell that, and almost to tell the teacher. Because I included all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you the only Jewish girl in the class?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, no, there were lots of Jewish girls. But none of them stood up and said anything. None of them have taken part in the discussion. I even expected the religious teacher, that he would participate in that problem and help me get back to school, because I felt I was totally provoked. But, of course, his salary depended on the school management, and it was public school. So he wouldn't have said anything. That was the general attitude that we have put up with anything and everything-- kept quiet. Never-- to any antisemitic remark, we would never respond. The procedure was to hide, to run away. If stones were thrown, you said, 'thank you' and you ran, and glad that the stone didn't hit you. And if it did, too bad. But I wasn't brought up that way. My father was also six-footer-- six foot two, and I could see that he stood up for himself. So I  had a role model. Even if they thought I didn't receive all that-- I didn't perceive everything what was going on at home-- I absorbed everything. My parents spoke very often German because they were, during the war, under Austrian-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] under Austrian government. So, especially if they didn't want us to understand, they spoke German, and that helped us to learn the language.

speaker: interviewer
text: Apart from that incident at school, were you aware of any antisemitism in Poland before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes, constantly. That was everyday occurrence-- here, there, everywhere. But we sort of-- like on a duck's back. It wasn't that important, unless it came close to the home-- it concerned me or concerned somebody close to me. Well, that did hurt very much. But that was nothing new. Absolutely nothing new.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you belong to any Jewish organizations?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wouldn't say I belonged, but I did go to many, because that was Saturday afternoon. We had no television, and that was entertainment. I would go to Bnei Akiva. We were gathering on the river Wisla. And there was a picnic. And there were girls and boys. And I would say I already sort of noticed boys. And if that afternoon finished, then we went to Shomer Hatzair with another girlfriend, because the boys were already more grown-up, and that was a more serious organization. And that I wasn't allowed to go to, because the boys were also older. But I did. I was tall, and I was accepted. And the afternoon tea was good, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we always sort of travelled from one-- there was Dror. I remember occasionally I would go there, to see what's going there. Actively I was too young, really, to belong. And we had a lot of Judaism at home, so my parents did not particularly care that I do belong to any organization. I did get the upbringing-- the Jewish upbringing-- at home.

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister and my brother went to Hebrew Gymnasium, so I even had a smattering of Hebrew. Today when I go to Israel, my friends who knew me from before the war are amazed how much Hebrew I understand and how much Hebrew I can speak. So that was pleasant.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your schooling was interrupted.

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: You were saying that your schooling was interrupted when war broke out.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's correct.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about the day that war break out?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I remember on the very day that war-- the Germans [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] dropped bombs very close to us, on a bridge on which there were trains going with the soldiers travelling. I wouldn't know exactly which direction, because by the time I reached the bridge and saw what was happening, it was already-- the bridge-- the train was standing. And there was a lot of blood and a lot of crying and a lot of dead people. But my mother was a nurse during the First World War. She took lots of sheets and tried to bandage them and helped them the best she knew how. And later, it was cordoned off, and we were told to go. And my mother always used to tell me, go home. So I-- we did go home, eventually, and a proper ambulance came to help and to take them away. But the Germans did a lot of damage on that very first day. And the distance from my home to that bridge was perhaps half a kilometer-- not further than that. So we saw the planes-- whoosh-- swooping down on the bridge. We saw the bombs falling.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Terrified! Terrified. You know, I sort of-- I always had the attitude to accept these things. Even then, as bad as it was, we accept it. The next thing-- I remember that more planes came the same day, in different spots. They were very well-informed where are the army barracks, where to bomb, what to bomb, and what not to bomb. Actually, Cracow is a beautiful city. It has not been destroyed. There is a beautiful palace-- Wawel. To this day, it's standing in its glory. And the bridges were preserved, except that bridge on which the train was traveling. It was important to them to destroy the soldiers rather than the bridge. A few days later, maybe the following day, my parents decided that we've got to run, to get out of the city. They thought that that will continue. And everybody was told to take a few things into a little case. And we went-- we ran. We ran faster on foot than the Germans were approaching in trucks and in tanks. We really ran. It took a long time for them to catch up with us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: We went towards [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Russian border. But our aim was to go to my grandparents, who lived very close to Przeworsk in a place called Siedleczka.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. S-I-E-D-L-E-C-Z-K-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Przeworsk-- can you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: And Przeworsk-- P-R-Z-E-W-O-R-S-K.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were heading for that place because your grandparents were there.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's correct. Yes. And they lived deeper in Galicia. And we thought, well, we will wait for whatever will happen down there. We will have food. Where in Podgorze, in Cracow, where we lived, you bought enough food for the day. There were shops. You did not have a storage to put things away, like one does here. People have a cellar full of wine. Well, we kept coal in the cellar. And anyhow, nobody was buying flour and sugar in those quantities to preserve for months. And we thought that that will take years, the war, so best we will be in the village.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did the whole family go?

speaker: interviewee
text: The whole family. My duty was to have a tiny little suitcase-- to carry that suitcase, which was full of chocolate-- [? Suchard ?] and all sorts of chocolate. In the house where we lived, my uncle had a konditorei. So that was my duty. And my poor parents always realized that I was left in the back because I kept opening the thing and nibbling at the chocolate. I was still-- as tall as I was, I think I was very immature for my age, then. Moments were that I understood everything what was going on. But the attitude of my parents was not to share anything-- any problems-- with the children. What I understood in German, or grasped a little bit from the Polish conversation, that I have known.

speaker: interviewer
text: But not-- children, please come together. We're going away. We have to run so the Germans don't get at us. None of that. I just remember the-- the incidents that we have found ourselves going. Where What for? Nobody told us. Only later I surmised that that's where we were heading-- to my grandparents. How did you travel?

speaker: interviewee
text: By foot, most of the time. Occasionally my father begged somebody to allow me to go onto a horse-drawn wagon for an hour or two. But I was very good at walking. I didn't mind. And it-- we were really-- as fast as we were trying to go, the amount of people that were going didn't allow us to go very fast. Because there were cars, bicycles, and the whole road was taken. So if you wanted to overtake somebody, you had to go into the ditch, go out, and be a few people ahead. And that didn't get anywhere. There were no trains.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the road-- was the road bombed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Absolutely. Every so often, we had to go into the woods or into the ditch to hide from the planes. And they were bombing. Unfortunately, if the horse was killed-- I think one was very concerned about those horses, because they have provided the means of transporting what everyone had. We didn't have a carriage. We didn't have a horse. But we always managed to attach ourself to another carriage. You know, you hold on to that wagon, and walk with it, and it's easier. And you sort of acquired a certain speed. And in the evening, we would go into the forest and try to sleep a little bit. Occasionally, if somebody came with a torch, with a light, oh, that for sure was spy. So it was a lot of that going on. And I think we were in this forest, Debica-- not terribly far. Occasionally, we would go into a farmer's house to sleep and get something to eat. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But that's how it was. And if the luggage was too much, the suitcase was too much, well, we just left it and went ahead without it. As the Germans were approaching, people always used to come, and soldiers on horses, telling us that the Germans are in such-and-such a place which was not too far from us.

speaker: interviewer
text: These were Polish soldiers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Polish soldiers, yes. Yes. Now, when I look back upon it, I think they were running away, too. That they didn't come to tell us, really, where the German soldiers were already. But, more or less, running ahead, because there was no hope to fight. All they had was a horse and a bayonet or szabla. How is szabla in--

speaker: interviewer
text: Sword?

speaker: interviewee
text: A sword, yes. Maybe gun. But they had guns. They had tanks. They were equipped extremely well. So the poor Polish soldiers didn't stand a chance. They were not equipped for war.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how long did you stay in Debica?

speaker: interviewee
text: Long enough to get very, very sick. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Because we ate whatever we could find, and wherever it was. And, of course, very soon we developed cholera. A lot of people suffered with cholera.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the parents, by that time, already knew that the Germans were in Cracow, and they didn't do anything to the civilians. So people started to go back. And I remember we went back by train.

speaker: interviewee
text: With us, we had also a few children from orphanage. My parents were active in welfare society. So the orphanage was divided amongst different people quickly, to take the children with us, and we did. On the train back home, we have had the children with us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And somehow, one in particular looked very Jewish. So in that train, already, going back to Cracow, one of the Poles spoke up to my father, considering him a non-Jew-- why do you take that boy back with you? What are you, a Jewish uncle? That was the expression. So my father let him have it. Ended peacefully. We did reach back home-- [INAUDIBLE]. But I remember I was very sick. I went to bed. And I had diarrhea, with blood. So my mother was again treating me with alcohol, wiping me with alcohol-- whole body, to get red of the infection.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we had a little dog, which also was with us on the running trip. And he did get infection, and he died. It was a lovely little sausage dog. So those little things were important, then.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about life in Cracow in those first, early times of the occupation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I liked very much to go with my mother to town. And somehow, on many occasions-- there was a very old synagogue called the Remuh synagogue. And when there was my sister exams or some other important business deal, my mother made a point of going to that Remuh synagogue, to the cemetery-- and there is a history attached to that cemetery-- and light a candle or yahrtzeit light.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you explain yahrtzeit?

speaker: interviewee
text: I enjoyed-- yahrtzeit is a memorial light which you light after a dead person, or if you have a certain [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] wish you ask God. And in-- and this is like a-- not sacrifice, but you light the light to-- I guess so God would see the prayer better. The wish would be granted. Terrible thing to say that, isn't it? But we were very much believers, certainly my mother. It was in a great sincerity. And I was always taken to that Remuh synagogue. [? That was ?] most of our excursions. Don't remember going to the pictures.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're just going to stop there for a moment to change the tape.
